coredata.dc:title: Sodium selenite-carbon dots nanocomposites enhance acaricidal activity of fenpropathrin: Mechanism and application

coredata.dc:description: As an essential trace element, selenium can be used to protect crops from pests, while, in nature, most crops cannot accumulate enough selenium from the soil to reach the effective dose for pest control. In this study, carbon dots modified with arginine in nano-scale was prepared and characterized, then, it was combined with sodium selenite to form selenium-carbon dots (Se-CDs). Function evaluation of Se-CDs showed that it could increase the absorption of selenium in plant leaves, promote the control efficiency of fenpropathrin, and protect plant from damage caused by Tetranychus cinnabarinus. In addition, we found that expressions of P450 genes and activity of P450 enzyme both decreased in selenium treated mites. In vivo, the acaricidal activity of fenpropathrin increased significantly when one of the P450 genes, CYP389B1, was silenced, and the recombinant protein of CYP389B1 could metabolize fenpropathrin in vitro. The results suggested that inhibiting the expression of P450 gene and repressing the detoxification of T. cinnabarinus was the molecular mechanism that how selenium promoted the acaricidal activity of fenpropathrin. The application of Se-CDs in the field will decrease the use of chemicals acaricides, reduce chemical residues, and ensure the safety of agricultural products.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an essential trace element, selenium can be used to protect crops from pests, while, in nature, most crops cannot accumulate enough selenium from the soil to reach the effective dose for pest control. In this study, carbon dots modified with arginine in nano-scale was prepared and characterized, then, it was combined with sodium selenite to form selenium-carbon dots (Se-CDs). Function evaluation of Se-CDs showed that it could increase the absorption of selenium in plant leaves, promote the control efficiency of fenpropathrin, and protect plant from damage caused by Tetranychus cinnabarinus. In addition, we found that expressions of P450 genes and activity of P450 enzyme both decreased in selenium treated mites. In vivo, the acaricidal activity of fenpropathrin increased significantly when one of the P450 genes, CYP389B1, was silenced, and the recombinant protein of CYP389B1 could metabolize fenpropathrin in vitro. The results suggested that inhibiting the expression of P450 gene and repressing the detoxification of T. cinnabarinus was the molecular mechanism that how selenium promoted the acaricidal activity of fenpropathrin. The application of Se-CDs in the field will decrease the use of chemicals acaricides, reduce chemical residues, and ensure the safety of agricultural products.

ce:keyword.ce:text: a commercially available sodium selenite preparation

ce:section.ce:para: Selenium is an essential trace element in plants, arthropods and other organisms. From the aspect of human health, selenium has many biological effects, such as regulating hormone secretion, antagonizing heavy metals, scavenging free radicals, and reducing cancer risk (Ewa and Edyta, 2017; Newman et al., 2019; Tobe and Mihara, 2018). Research of ovarian cancer indicated that selenium compounds could prevent the occurrence of cancer cell resistance (Caffrey and Frenkel, 2000). In plant protection, selenium in plants showed toxicity to pests and could act as a repellent of herbivores (De La Riva and Trumble, 2016; Trumble and Sorensen, 2008). Pests, such as Frankliniella occidentalis and Tetranychus urticae, preferred plants with lower content of selenium, and certain content of selenium in plants could result in decrease of population growth rate of these pests (Quinn et al., 2010). Thus, selenium has great potential to be used in pest controls. However, investigation on effects of selenium on susceptibility of pests to chemical pesticides lags behind. Since fenpropathrin is usually used for leaf spraying, and sodium selenite is usually supplied from the roots, in order to use the two together, it is necessary to change one of the application method, therefore, development of an efficient method to increase absorption of selenium in plant leaf (Lima et al., 2018; Longchamp et al., 2015; White, 2018). This is the first step for the combined application of the fenpropathrin and selenium.

Nanoparticles acting as drug carriers can not only improve drug intake, increase drug accumulation, but also increase drug bioavailability (Li et al., 2018; Mandal and Jana, 2018). A multifunctional drug delivery system based on MoS2 nanoparticles could effectively deliver doxorubicin (DOX) to MCF-7 cells to inhibit the growth of breast tumor cells (Yang et al., 2020). Mesoporous silica nanoparticles also exhibited a good characteristic to deliver DOX to cancer cells to enhance drug efficacy (Chen et al., 2018). Recent study demonstrated that magnetic nanoparticles as a carrier could delivered foreign DNA into pollen (Zhao et al., 2017). In mite control, nanoparticles are also a good choice for delivery of chemical acaricides (Peng et al., 2019).

As a new type of fluorescent nanoparticles (less than 10 nm), carbon dots (CDs) have drawn much attention due to its small size, good biocompatibility, easy synthesis, and low toxicity (Han et al., 2018). Usually, the CDs consists of two parts: carbon nucleus and functional groups. The inner carbon nucleus is a carbon skeleton composed of many SP2 hybrids and SP3 hybrids. The exterior is composed of various functional groups. Most functional groups (like hydroxyl, carboxyl, amine, etc.) on surface of CDs show excellent performance in drug delivery and photosensitizers (Tian and Yin, 2019). The fluorescent carbon nanoparticles set a new direction for nanomaterials that have now been widely used in many fields (Xu et al., 2004). In this study, we intend to use CDs as a carrier to enhance the absorption of selenium in plant leaves, and evaluated the effect of selenium and fenpropathrin in the control of Tetranychus cinnabarinus, which had become highly resistant to this pesticide in China (Chen et al., 2012). The purposes of this experiment were to optimize the way of selenium supplementation and to explore its role in pest control. Our findings could contribute greatly to reducing the tolerance of pests to pesticides by establishing an innovative method as well as to provide the important supplement using nano-enabled techniques in the sustainable agriculture.

The T. cinnabarinus, which were collected from the fields of Beibei, Chongqing, were kept indoors and were not exposed to pesticides until the following experiments. The rearing conditions were set as: 26 ± 1 °C temperature, 55–75% relative humidity (RH) and a 14 h light (L): 10 h dark (D) photoperiod.
The 92% fenpropathrin was purchased from Bangnong Chemical Company (Guangzhou, China) and the 97% sodium selenite (Na2SeO3) was from Shanghai Tyrell Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of amine-capped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The solution containing 1.0507 g citric acid, 335 μL ethylenediamine, and 10 mL double distilled water (ddH2O) was transferred to a 50 mL flask and heated at 200 °C overnight. Then, the solution was cooled to ambient temperature on the bench (Bourlinos et al., 2015; Qian et al., 2018; Qu et al., 2012). The reaction end-product was brown-black and transparent, and CDs was obtained after dialysis. The production yield was 50%. Subsequently, 1 mL of 20% arginine solution was added to the CDs solution for 4 h at 80 °C to modify the surface of CDs by arginine. The excess arginine was removed using a dialysis bag and then the modified CD solution was dried by a vacuum freeze dryer.

Size distribution of the synthesized particles was measured by dynamic light scattering apparatus (DLS; Zetasizer Nano ZS; Malvern). Fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-2700; Hitachi Co., Tokyo, Japan) was used to detect fluorescence FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used to analyze IR spectra. High-resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) was carried out on a JEM-2010 (HR) microscope at voltage 200 kV (Shimadzu). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (AXIS UltraDLD, Kratos, Japan).was used to analyze the basic composition of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading Na2SeO3 on CDs and uptaking of selenium-carbon dots (Se-CDs) for cowpea
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were disperse in ddH2O and mixed with Na2SeO3 based on a volume ratio of 1:1 (v/v), and the final concentration of CDs was set as 100 mg·L−1. Mix Na2SeO3 with the CDs solution, the final concentration of sodium selenite in the solution was 0, 1.85, 5.56, 16.67, 50, 150, 450, 1350 mM, respectively. Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectroscopy (UV–Vis) detection test was carried out at 200–300 nm using a microplate reader (EON, BioTek Instruments Inc., Winooski, Vermont, USA), and the step wavelength was 1 nm. Fluorescence spectrometry was measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer with 370–570 nm emission (10 nm slit width) and 350 nm excitation (10 nm slit width) wavelength at 25 °C. Each group of treatments was a curve obtained by subtracting the background (ddH2O), using the wavelength as the abscissa and the fluorescence intensity as the ordinate. The same eight concentrations (0–1350 mM) were used to test the plant's ability to absorb the mixture of sodium selenite and CDs. Putting the cowpea seedlings into eight concentrations of solution and cultivate for 7 days. There are three biological replicates in each treatment.

For the biological evaluation of effects of the Se-CDs solution, selecting cowpea, which was the main host crops for T. cinnabarinus, as the test objects. The Na2SeO3 (150 mM) and CDs mixed solution that was optimized in the fluorescence spectrum experiment was diluted by 6000, 12,000, 25,000, 50,000, 100,000, and 20,000 times and then sprayed on cowpea leaves. After 7 days, physiological changes of the cowpea leaves, plant height, and root length were recorded. The water treatment was set as the control and three biological replicates in each treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of the total selenium content
ce:section.ce:para: The cowpeas were treated with Se and Se-CDs. After 3 days, wash the residues on the surface of the leaves with clean water. Subsequently, the concentration of selenium in the leaves was determined by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (iCAPQ, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) (Adams et al., 2002; Zhao et al., 2007). A microwave digester (ETHOS 1, MILESTONE, Italy) was used to fully digest approximately 2 g of the cowpeas leaves with 5 mL of nitric acid. After diluting the digestant to 50 mL with DI water prior to measure the total selenium content using ICP-MS. The instrument parameters were as follows: RF power: 1550 W, pump speed: 40 rpm, S/C Temperature: 2.7 °C, magnification depth: 5 mm, cold flow rate: 14 L·min−1, auxiliary flow rate: 0.8 L·min−1, atomizer flow rate: 0.9 L·min−1. Each treatment was repeated 3 times. The selenium standard solution was purchased from the National Nonferrous Metals and Electronic Materials Analysis and Testing Center (Beijing, China). In another experiment, the screening test for the concentration of sodium selenite was tested after treating cowpea with 4 concentrations of selenite (0, 5, 20, 50, 200 μM) for 3 days. Afterwards, the total selenium content of the leaves was tested. There were four biological replicates in each treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioassay of fenpropathrin for T. cinnabarinus and pot experiment about Se-CDs effect
ce:section.ce:para: The spray method was used for the bioassay of mites (Van Leeuwen et al., 2006). The mites were treated with a series of concentrations of fenpropathrin acetone solutions using potter spray tower. After 24 h, the mortality of mites was calculated and converted into a probability value. The concentration of fenpropathrin was taken as the abscissa, and the death probability of mites was taken as the ordinate to compare the difference between the Na2SeO3 treatment and control. There were three biological replicates in each treatment. The obtained data were used PoloPlus to calculate LC50. Statistical analysis of toxicity regression equations and the median lethal concentration (LC50) were calculated by probit analysis.

The greenhouse experiment was measured according to Peng et al. (2019). The cowpea seedlings were used as the target plant. A total of 100 female-adult T. cinnabarinus were transferred to each cowpea seedling. Three different types of selenium products were sprayed on the leaves together with fenpropathrin, including Na2SeO3 solution (Na2SeO3), Na2SeO3 nano-formulation (Se-CDs), and a commercially available Na2SeO3 preparation (SeC). The numbers of survived mites on the cowpea were recorded at day 1, 4, and 7. The applied concentration of fenpropathrin was 68 mg·L−1, which was set as the control. The control effects against mites were calculated as follows:
(1)

Ta: survival number after treatment in the treatment group;
Tb: survival number before treatment in the treatment group;
Ca: survival number after treatment in the control group;
Cb: survival number before treatment in the control group.

About 50 adult T. cinnabarinus were transferred to single-leaf cowpea seedlings for leaf damage evaluation. The cowpeas were treated in advance with the same method and concentration as the indoor pot experiment. After 3 days of feeding, The T. cinnabarinus were picked up with a brush. Scanning the leaf sample and save it as a picture (Zhu et al., 2020). Then, using the ImageJ software (National Institutes of Health and LOCI, USA) to statistics the damaged area and total area of cowpea leaves (Pixel2). The damaged leaves would show chlorosis and white spots, and the software would distinguish the color differences on the leaves. The Leaf damage area ratio was calculated as follows:
(2)

Measurements of cytochrome P450s (P450s), glutathione S-transferases (GSTs), and carboxylesterases (CarEs) activity was used as described in previous studies (Feng et al., 2019; Shi et al., 2016; Wei et al., 2019). In a low temperature environment, T. cinnabarinus were homogenized in buffer solution, respectively (P450s: 0.1 M phosphatic buffer solution (PBS); GSTs: 0.5 M Tris-HCL; CarEs: 0.04 M PBS), then centrifuge at 10,000g for 10 min. The supernatant was the enzyme solution that waiting to be tested. The determination of P450 enzyme activity used p-nitroanisole as the substrate, and detected the OD value at 400 nm. The determination of GST enzyme activity used 2,4-dinitrochlorobenzene as the substrate and detected the OD values that were recorded every 30 s over a 5 min period at 340 nm. The determination of CarE enzyme activity used the 3 × 10−4 M substrate, which prepared by 0.03 M 1-naphthyl acetate, 10−4 M physostigmine and 0.04 M PH 7.0 PBS. The mixture was incubated at 30 °C for 10 min and records the OD value at 600 nm. There were three biological replicates in each treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative Real-time PCR (qPCR) and RNA interference (RNAi) of P450s
ce:section.ce:para: The method of qPCR and RNAi was described in a previous other's study, and a list of primers is shown in Table S1 (Hu et al., 2019). The complementary DNA (cDNA) was used as a template to mix with IQ™ SYBR Green Supermix and primers. The results of qPCR were calculated using the 2−ΔΔCt method (Sun et al., 2010). The T7 RiboMAX™ Express RNAi System kit (Promega, Madison, USA) was used to synthesize siRNA. The interference was carried out by leaf-disc feeding method with 20 μg siRNA per leaf. siRNA-CYP389B1 was used for treatment and siRNA-GFP was used as control. The detection of expression level, mortality and enzyme activity after RNAi refers to the previous experimental steps. There were three biological replicates in each treatment.

The method of qPCR and RNAi was described in a previous other's study, and a list of primers is shown in Table S1 (Hu et al., 2019). The complementary DNA (cDNA) was used as a template to mix with IQ™ SYBR Green Supermix and primers. The results of qPCR were calculated using the 2−ΔΔCt method (Sun et al., 2010). The T7 RiboMAX™ Express RNAi System kit (Promega, Madison, USA) was used to synthesize siRNA. The interference was carried out by leaf-disc feeding method with 20 μg siRNA per leaf. siRNA-CYP389B1 was used for treatment and siRNA-GFP was used as control. The detection of expression level, mortality and enzyme activity after RNAi refers to the previous experimental steps. There were three biological replicates in each treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Prokaryotic expression and metabolism analysis of CYP389B1
ce:section.ce:para: The recombinant protein was provided with suitable conditions to metabolize with fenpropathrin for 4 h in vitro, and then gas chromatography (GC, Agilent 6890, USA) was used to detect the remaining fenpropathrin in the system (Demaeght et al., 2013). The gas chromatography detection used HP-5 capillary column and micro-ECD detector. Sample injection mode was non-fractionation, the carrier gas was N2, and the flow rate was 50 mL·min−1. The furnace temperature was programmed to increase the temperature to 80 °C for 30 s, then the temperature was increased to 120 °C at 7 °C·min−1, then the temperature was continuous to increase to 280 °C at 10 °C·min−1 and kept for 10 min. The reaction system includes 2 μg fenpropathrin, recombinant protein (10 μg or 20 μg) and 0.2 μM nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADPH), and the same reaction system only without recombinant protein was used as control. There were three biological replicates in each treatment.

The SPSS 20.0 (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA) was used to analyze the significance of the difference. The GraphPad Prism 8 (GraphPad Software, San Diego, California) and Excel 2010 (Microsoft Corporation, USA) were used to make graph and table respectively. Photoshop (Adobe Systems Incorporated, USA) was utilized to optimize the picture. All data indicated mean values ± standard errors of the mean (SEM). The t-test was used to analyze the difference between treatments (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01).

HR-TEM image shows that the CDs were uniform, monodispersed sphere or hemisphere particles (Fig. 1a). The size ranged between 2 and 4 nm (Fig. 1a embedded diagram), and the CDs showed a bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light (Fig. 1b). Infrared spectrum of the hydrophilic CDs is shown in Fig. 1c. For CDs-arginine, the broad bands at 3410 cm−1 (νN-H) and 1575 cm−1 (δN-H) were attributed to the vibration of -NH2 groups. The peak of 1707 cm−1 was attributed to stretching vibration of -C=O and the peak of 1240 cm−1 was attributed to stretching of C-O-C (Fig. 1c).

XPS measurements were carried out to establish the elemental composition of CDs (Fig. 1). The survey spectrum showed N1s peak at 399 eV, C1s peak at 284 eV, and an O1s peak at 532 eV (Fig. 2a), which confirms the successful formation of CDs. Also, results of C1s spectrum X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) of CDs showed major groups (284.5 eV CC, 288.3 eV -(-C=O, -NH2, 286.0 eV CN etc.)) in the arginine in the presence of CDs (Fig. 2b). The N1s spectrum of CDs showed two peaks at 399.9 eV and 401.6 eV, which correspond to C-N-C and NH, respectively (Fig. 2c). The results suggested that the arginine was linked to the CDs surface. Previous studies demonstrated that the –COOH on the surface of CD synthesized from urea and citric acid could non-covalently bond with -NH2 on DOX cancer drugs to form CD-DOX drug conjugates, and successfully delivered DOX into HepG2 cancer cells (Zeng et al., 2016). If hollow carbon dots were synthesized, the van der Waals force of the surface functional groups could also be used to load DOX (Wang et al., 2013). The amine groups on the surface of the carbon dots were found conducive for drug delivery (Zhou et al., 2020). The surface of the synthesized carbon dots in this experiment also contained arginine, which has amine and carboxyl groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading Na2SeO3 on CDs to form Se-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: With increasing of concentration of Na2SeO3 in the mixture of Na2SeO3 and carbon dots (from 0 to 1350 mM), CDs had an absorption red shift (Fig. 3 a). In addition, the Na2SeO3 and blue fluorescent CDs would form a non-luminous complex. The quenching phenomenon was obviously related to the concentration of Na2SeO3 (Fig. 3b). When the final concentration of Na2SeO3 reached 150 mM, the fluorescence value of Na2SeO3 stabilized (Fig. 3b embedded diagram). In order to maximize the use of this reagent, the mixture of selenium and carbon dots (Se-CDs), was prepared with a final concentration of CDs at 100 mg·L−1 and Na2SeO3 at 150 mM. In the in vivo verification of Se-CDs, although Na2SeO3 greater than 16.67 mM caused obvious damage and even death on cowpea, the fluorescence in the leaves was quenched at 16.67 mM, which was also consistent with the result of fluorescence (Fig. S1). Therefore, it can be concluded that CDs could combine with Na2SeO3 and carry it into cowpeas leaves.

With increasing of concentration of Na2SeO3 in the mixture of Na2SeO3 and carbon dots (from 0 to 1350 mM), CDs had an absorption red shift (Fig. 3a). In addition, the Na2SeO3 and blue fluorescent CDs would form a non-luminous complex. The quenching phenomenon was obviously related to the concentration of Na2SeO3 (Fig. 3b). When the final concentration of Na2SeO3 reached 150 mM, the fluorescence value of Na2SeO3 stabilized (Fig. 3b embedded diagram). In order to maximize the use of this reagent, the mixture of selenium and carbon dots (Se-CDs), was prepared with a final concentration of CDs at 100 mg·L−1 and Na2SeO3 at 150 mM. In the in vivo verification of Se-CDs, although Na2SeO3 greater than 16.67 mM caused obvious damage and even death on cowpea, the fluorescence in the leaves was quenched at 16.67 mM, which was also consistent with the result of fluorescence (Fig. S1). Therefore, it can be concluded that CDs could combine with Na2SeO3 and carry it into cowpeas leaves.

According to the previous experiment, it was found that the selected Se-CDs in which the concentration of sodium selenite was 150 mM exceeded the maximum tolerance of the plant, so they were diluted. The mixture was diluted to 6000, 12,000, 25,000, 50,000, 100,000, 200,000 times, and the concentration of Na2SeO3 contained in the solution was 0.75, 1.5, 3, 6, 12.5, 25 μM, respectively. Foliar exposure of different concentrations of Se-CDs had no significant impact on plant height and root length of cowpea (Fig. 4a and b). However, high concentration of Se-CDs (dilution ratio ≤ 25,000 times) caused damage on cowpea leaves (Fig. 4c). Thus, a mixture diluted at 50000 times was selected for the subsequent experiment.

In other words, this formation was able to promote the selenium absorption in plant leaf, and made leaf spray as an alternative of root irrigation. The CDs could successfully load with Na2SeO3 mainly because the positively charged functional groups on the surface were combined with selenite through electrostatic adsorption. The particle diameter of the carbon dots particles were increased due to the incorporation of selenium. As a result, the conjugated π-domain structure of the carbon dots was increased, the π-π* electron energy level was narrowed, and the emission peak of the carbon dots was red-shifted (Molaei, 2019). And the phenomenon of fluorescence quenching and red shift could increase with increasing the concentration of sodium selenite. It was demonstrated that the CD-Oxa complex, a new theranostic nanomedicine, was synthesized by dehydration condensation of amine groups and carboxyl groups on the surface of carbon dots. It could successfully monitor the migration of drugs in organisms and the delivery of drugs (Zheng et al., 2014). Similarly, through electrostatic adsorption, hydroxyl functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQD) could be combined with cationic acetylated dendrimer to form a fluorescent hybrid, CQDs@G5-Ac85, to monitor the cell distribution of the anticancer drug epirubicin in MCF-7 cells (Matai et al., 2015). Therefore, it is inevitable that CDs can successfully deliver sodium selenite into the leaves in this experiment.

Se content in cowpea leaves was measured upon exposure to Na2SeO3 solution and Se-CDs via root and foliar route (Fig. S2). For the root exposure, the selenium content in cowpea leaves was no difference between Na2SeO3 solution and Se-CDs (Fig. S2a). While using the foliar application, the Se-CDs significantly improved the selenium absorption in cowpea leaves, indicating that the application of Se-CDs could promote the selenium absorption in leaves (Fig. S2b). Lanza et al. (2021) reported that exposure to 1–10 mg·kg−1 selenium enhanced cowpea growth. However, our results suggest that the selenium content in cowpea leaves treated with Se-CDs was only 0.14 mg·kg−1 (Fig. S2b). Thus, it is reasonable that the promoting impacts on cowpea growth were not notable (Fig. 4). Selenium has shown excellent performance in pest control, crop enhancement, radicals scavenging and nutrient supplement. If the concentration of selenium could be further optimized, it might be able to apply as a selenium supplement in crops.

Se content in cowpea leaves was measured upon exposure to Na2SeO3 solution and Se-CDs via root and foliar route (Fig. S2). For the root exposure, the selenium content in cowpea leaves was no difference between Na2SeO3 solution and Se-CDs (Fig. S2a). While using the foliar application, the Se-CDs significantly improved the selenium absorption in cowpea leaves, indicating that the application of Se-CDs could promote the selenium absorption in leaves (Fig. S2b). Lanza et al. (2021) reported that exposure to 1–10 mg·kg−1 selenium enhanced cowpea growth. However, our results suggest that the selenium content in cowpea leaves treated with Se-CDs was only 0.14 mg·kg−1 (Fig. S2b). Thus, it is reasonable that the promoting impacts on cowpea growth were not notable (Fig. 4). Selenium has shown excellent performance in pest control, crop enhancement, radicals scavenging and nutrient supplement. If the concentration of selenium could be further optimized, it might be able to apply as a selenium supplement in crops.

In order to eliminate the interference of other factors, only Na2SeO3 was used to treat cowpea seedlings to assess the selenium effects on susceptibility of T. cinnabarinus to fenpropathrin. When the concentration of Na2SeO3 was 20 μM, the absorption capacity of cowpea leaves reached saturation, and 20 μM Na2SeO3 had no obvious lethal effects to T. cinnabarinus (Fig. S3). When spraying with different concentrations of fenpropathrin, the mortalities of T. cinnabarinus treated with 20 μM Na2SeO3 were significantly higher than that of control (Fig. 5a). The LC50 of T. cinnabarinus was reduced from 746.77 mg·L−1 to 332.19 mg·L−1, meant Na2SeO3 enhanced 2.25-fold toxicity of fenpropathrin against T. cinnabarinus (Table 1). Generally, fenpropathrin was used by spraying, while Na2SeO3 was used by root irrigation. This difference is the main obstacle for combined application of fenpropathrin and Na2SeO3. If Na2SeO3 was still used in the traditional way to increase the acaricidal effect of fenpropathrin, it would be inconvenient for practice and accumulation of Na2SeO3 in the leaves. In order to achieve the purpose of applying Na2SeO3 and fenpropathrin simultaneously, CDs was selected to deliver Na2SeO3 for leaf spraying.

Effects of prepared Se-CDs on control of T. cinnabarinus were evaluated by assessment of leaf damage area ratio and indoor pot experiment. In comparison with the Na2SeO3 solution treatment, the statistical result of damage area indicates that Se-CDs stimulated the defense mechanisms in cowpea leaves (Figs. S4 and 5b). Selenium was recommended as a new type of protective agent in agricultural production practice, due to its toxic and repellent effects (Mechora, 2019). Aphids can avoid taking sodium selenite less than 10 mg Se kg−1 d. wt., if Se was more than 10 mg Se kg−1 d. wt., it will die (Hanson et al., 2004). So it also showed a good miticidal effect in the leaf damage experiment. In the greenhouse experiment, exposure to three types (Na2SeO3, Se-CDs, and SeC) of selenium products exhibited a synergistic effect with fenpropathrin after 1 day of treatment, among which Na2SeO3 was the most obvious. At Day 4, the synergistic effect of Se-CDs was significantly stronger than that of Na2SeO3. At Day 7, the synergistic effect was only evident in the treatment with Se-CDs. Compared with control, the control effect of fenpropathrin was increased by 36%. Compared with Se, the control effect of fenpropathrin was increased by 26% (Table 2 ).

Effects of prepared Se-CDs on control of T. cinnabarinus were evaluated by assessment of leaf damage area ratio and indoor pot experiment. In comparison with the Na2SeO3 solution treatment, the statistical result of damage area indicates that Se-CDs stimulated the defense mechanisms in cowpea leaves (Figs. S4 and 5b). Selenium was recommended as a new type of protective agent in agricultural production practice, due to its toxic and repellent effects (Mechora, 2019). Aphids can avoid taking sodium selenite less than 10 mg Se kg−1 d. wt., if Se was more than 10 mg Se kg−1 d. wt., it will die (Hanson et al., 2004). So it also showed a good miticidal effect in the leaf damage experiment. In the greenhouse experiment, exposure to three types (Na2SeO3, Se-CDs, and SeC) of selenium products exhibited a synergistic effect with fenpropathrin after 1 day of treatment, among which Na2SeO3 was the most obvious. At Day 4, the synergistic effect of Se-CDs was significantly stronger than that of Na2SeO3. At Day 7, the synergistic effect was only evident in the treatment with Se-CDs. Compared with control, the control effect of fenpropathrin was increased by 36%. Compared with Se, the control effect of fenpropathrin was increased by 26% (Table 2).

The results of bioassay and indoor pot experiment have demonstrated that selenium has a significant synergism on the toxicity of fenpropathrin, and the formulation of Se-CDs makes the field applications more possible. This study suggests Se-CDs can enhance plant defense against pests, and reduce the usage rate of chemical acaricides. The combination of Se-CDs and chemical acaricide can increase the control effect of T. cinnabarinus. In fact, selenium nanoparticles were easier to absorb than the ionic form of selenium (Golubkina et al., 2017). The rapid evolution of pest resistance has brought tremendous pressure to the development of new pesticides (Caffrey and Frenkel, 2013). Treatment with Se could reduce the level of Rad51-AP1 protein, making cells more sensitive to carboplatin (Rodriguezrodriguez et al., 2014). Metal-based nanoparticles, including copper, zinc, silver, nickel and titanium dioxide, have shown excellent performance in preventing and controlling parasites in medicine and veterinary medicine (Banumathi et al., 2017; Benelli et al., 2017). For nano-encapsulated biological pesticides (carvacrol and linalool), they have excellent repellent, acaricidal and oviparicidal activities against Tetranychus urticae (Campos et al., 2018). The main findings in the present study suggest that enhancing mite sensitivity to acaricide is a new way to strengthen the control efficacy of traditional acaricides and alleviate the pressure on inventing new acaricide.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selenium inhibits the gene expression and activity of cytochrome P450
ce:section.ce:para: CYP389B1, the most down-regulation gene, was selected for functional verification. After silence CYP389B1 of T. cinnabarinus, the expression of CYP389B1 was reduced by 50.18% (Fig. 7 a), the activity of P450 was reduced by 31.38% (Fig. 7b), and the susceptibility of mites to fenpropathrin was increased by 51.49% (Fig. 7c). It is demonstrated that Na2SeO3 could inhibit the expression of CYP389B1, which was indeed related to the susceptibility of the T. cinnabarinus to fenpropathrin. In addition, the prokaryotic expression system was used to obtain the CYP389B1 recombinant protein of T. cinnabarinus in vitro (Fig. S5). The fenpropathrin could inhibit the activity of recombinant CYP389B1, the IC50 was 259.3 ± 116.1 μM, which meant fenpropathrin could bind to CYP389B1 protein directly (Fig. 7d). Importantly, 10 μg and 20 μg of recombinant protein could decompose 22.38% and 30.00% of fenpropathrin within 4 h, respectively (Fig. 7e). Thus, CYP389B1 plays an important role in the detoxification metabolism of fenpropathrin; additionally, selenium could increase the toxicity of fenpropathrin due to the inhibition of P450 activity, which leading to a decrease of fenpropathrin metabolism.

CYP389B1, the most down-regulation gene, was selected for functional verification. After silence CYP389B1 of T. cinnabarinus, the expression of CYP389B1 was reduced by 50.18% (Fig. 7a), the activity of P450 was reduced by 31.38% (Fig. 7b), and the susceptibility of mites to fenpropathrin was increased by 51.49% (Fig. 7c). It is demonstrated that Na2SeO3 could inhibit the expression of CYP389B1, which was indeed related to the susceptibility of the T. cinnabarinus to fenpropathrin. In addition, the prokaryotic expression system was used to obtain the CYP389B1 recombinant protein of T. cinnabarinus in vitro (Fig. S5). The fenpropathrin could inhibit the activity of recombinant CYP389B1, the IC50 was 259.3 ± 116.1 μM, which meant fenpropathrin could bind to CYP389B1 protein directly (Fig. 7d). Importantly, 10 μg and 20 μg of recombinant protein could decompose 22.38% and 30.00% of fenpropathrin within 4 h, respectively (Fig. 7e). Thus, CYP389B1 plays an important role in the detoxification metabolism of fenpropathrin; additionally, selenium could increase the toxicity of fenpropathrin due to the inhibition of P450 activity, which leading to a decrease of fenpropathrin metabolism.

The toxicity of insecticides is closely related to the detoxification enzyme activity of insect. Increased activity of the detoxification enzymes contributes to enhanced metabolism of insect and reduction of the toxicity of insecticides (Leeuwen et al., 2010). In addition, previous studies also reported that p450s were involved in the formation of resistance to most insecticide. In a rat study, it was also found that selenium reduced the activity and expression of CYP4501A in the liver (Peng et al., 2016). It also observed that the expression of CYP1A1 and CYP2E1 genes in the extralenticular tissues of rat were up-regulated and the expression of CYP3A was down-regulated upon exposure to selenium (Nakamura et al., 2007). These results suggest that Na2SeO3 could inhibit the expression level of P450 gene and subsequently increased the efficiency of pesticides to insects. For example, the sensitivity of brown planthopper (Nilaparvata lugens) to imidacloprid, the sensitivity of fruit flies to DDT, and the sensitivity of house flies to pyrethroids could all be increased upon exposure to Na2SeO3 (McCart and Ffrench-Constant, 2008; Pan et al., 2018; Pang et al., 2016).

In summary, we used carbon dots to successfully change the way of supplying sodium selenite from external sources. This leads to a decrease in the expression of CYP389B1, which is associated with fenpropathrin resistance, in T. cinnabarinus. Compared with the direct use of fenpropathrin, it increases the sensitivity of T. cinnabarinus to fenpropathrin, suggesting that Se-CDs as a way in managing the pest control could improve the sensitivity of pests to chemicals.
